text: How is the Nova Scotia Class 7 knowledge test divided?

options.text: A vision test and a road test

explanation: The Class 7 knowledge test has two parts: driving rules and road signs. Each part has 20 multiple-choice questions.

text: How many questions must you answer correctly to pass each part of the Class 7 knowledge test?
explanation: You need at least 16 correct answers on each 20-question part. Passing one part does not make up for failing the other.

text: How long do you have to complete each part of the online Class 7 knowledge test?
explanation: Each part of the knowledge test must be completed within 30 minutes. The full simulator uses that timing for each section.

text: What must happen after you pass the online Class 7 knowledge test before you can drive?

options.text: You must visit Registry of Motor Vehicles or Access Nova Scotia to finish licence requirements
You can drive immediately if you print the result
You only need to buy insurance
You must wait 30 days before applying

explanation: Passing the online test is not the licence by itself. You still need to complete the remaining learner licence requirements before driving.

text: What is the minimum age to apply for a Nova Scotia Learner’s Licence (Class 7)?
explanation: A person must be at least 16 to apply for a Class 7 Learner’s Licence. Drivers who are 16 or 17 need parental or guardian consent.

text: What blood alcohol level must a learner driver maintain in Nova Scotia?
explanation: Learner drivers must keep a zero blood alcohol level. This is stricter than the Criminal Code limit.

text: What does a steady red traffic light mean?

options.text: Stop only if traffic is coming
Slow down and proceed with caution

explanation: A red signal means all traffic facing it must stop at the marked stop line or before the crosswalk. Proceed only when legally allowed and safe.

text: What does a steady yellow or amber traffic light mean?

options.text: Stop if you can do so safely
Speed up to clear the intersection
The signal is out of service

explanation: A yellow or amber signal means you must stop before entering the intersection if you can stop safely. Never speed up to beat the red.

text: What does a green traffic light allow you to do?

options.text: Proceed when safe, yielding where required

explanation: A green light means you may go when safe, but you must still yield to pedestrians and vehicles already in the intersection.

text: What does a flashing red traffic light mean?

options.text: Stop, then proceed only when safe

explanation: A flashing red light has the same meaning as a stop sign. Stop completely and do not proceed until it is safe.

text: What does a flashing yellow or amber traffic light mean?

options.text: Slow down and proceed with caution
The signal applies only to pedestrians

explanation: A flashing yellow or amber light has the meaning of a warning sign. Slow down and continue carefully.

text: If traffic lights are not working at an intersection, what should drivers do?

options.text: Treat it as a multi-stop intersection
Drive through if you are on the larger road
Stop only if another car is present

explanation: When traffic signal lights are not working, treat the intersection as a multi-stop. Stop, then proceed when you have the right of way and it is safe.

text: Who has the right of way at a marked or unmarked crosswalk?
explanation: Drivers must yield to pedestrians at intersections, whether the crosswalk is marked or unmarked. Always be ready to stop.

text: At an intersection, what must a driver turning left on a green light do?

options.text: Yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians in the path
Stop until the light turns red

explanation: A driver turning left must yield to oncoming vehicles and pedestrians who may be in the path. Turn only when it is safe.

text: When two vehicles arrive at an uncontrolled intersection at the same time, who yields?

options.text: The driver on the left yields to the driver on the right
The driver on the right yields to the driver on the left
The vehicle going straight always yields

explanation: When two or more vehicles reach an intersection at the same time, the vehicle on the left must yield. Courtesy never replaces checking that it is safe.

text: What should you do when leaving a private driveway or parking lot?

options.text: Stop and yield to all vehicles and pedestrians
Yield only to vehicles from the left

explanation: Before entering a road from a driveway or parking lot, stop and yield to all road users. Move only when the way is clear.

text: Who has right of way when a vehicle is already in an intersection?

options.text: The vehicle already in the intersection
The vehicle that arrived later from the right
The vehicle with the newest licence

explanation: A vehicle already in the intersection has right of way over one that has not yet entered. You still must avoid a collision when possible.

text: Where should you position your front wheels while waiting to turn left?

options.text: Straight ahead until you can complete the turn
Turned left toward the oncoming lane

explanation: Keep your wheels straight while waiting to turn left. If hit from behind, turned wheels can push you into oncoming traffic.

text: When should you avoid changing lanes?

options.text: On a straight road with lane markings

explanation: Make lane changes before entering an intersection. Changing lanes in an intersection is unsafe and can confuse other drivers.

text: At a four-way stop, what should you do before proceeding?

options.text: Stop completely and go only when it is your turn and safe
Roll through if no one honks
Follow the closest vehicle without stopping
Proceed first if your vehicle is larger

explanation: At a multi-stop, every driver must stop completely and proceed by right-of-way order. If unsure, yield and communicate clearly.

text: What must you do if a pedestrian remains in the street when the signal changes?

options.text: Allow the pedestrian to finish crossing safely
Stop only if the pedestrian is in your lane

explanation: If a signal changes while a pedestrian is still crossing, drivers must allow the pedestrian to complete the crossing safely.

text: What should you do when a crossing guard holds a stop paddle toward your vehicle?

options.text: Stop and obey the crossing guard
Proceed if no child is visible

explanation: Drivers must obey appointed crossing guards. A stop paddle facing your vehicle means stop.

text: What is the safe choice when passing a cyclist?

options.text: Leave plenty of space and pass only when safe
Pass closely so you spend less time beside them
Pass on the shoulder whenever possible

explanation: Cyclists are vulnerable road users. Give space, check for oncoming traffic, and pass only when it can be done safely.

text: What should you do when a transit bus signals to re-enter traffic from a bus stop?

options.text: Yield unless you cannot do so safely
Speed up to pass before it moves
Stop for at least one minute

explanation: Nova Scotia requires other drivers to yield to a signalling transit bus when it is safe to do so. Be prepared for the bus to move out.
handbookSection: Driver’s Handbook Amendments - Yield to Transit Bus

text: What is the safest approach around large trucks?

options.text: Stay out of blind spots and leave extra room
Follow closely to reduce wind resistance
Assume the driver can always see you

explanation: Large vehicles have bigger blind spots and need more space to stop and turn. Keep visible and avoid cutting in.

text: When meeting farm or slow-moving equipment, what should you do?

options.text: Slow down and pass only when legal and safe
Flash headlights until it moves off the road

explanation: Slow-moving vehicles may turn wide or move unpredictably. Reduce speed and pass only when you have clear sight distance and it is legal.

text: What should you do when an emergency vehicle approaches with flashing lights and siren?

options.text: Move to the right edge or curb and stop
Stop in the middle of the lane

explanation: When an emergency vehicle approaches, move to the right edge or curb and stop completely. Stay stopped until it passes or an officer directs you.

text: How close may you follow an emergency fire vehicle responding to a call?
explanation: You must never follow an emergency fire vehicle within 150 metres. Leave the route clear for emergency response.

text: How close may you drive or park to an emergency vehicle stopped to assist at a collision?
explanation: The handbook says never drive or park within 150 metres of an emergency vehicle stopped to investigate a collision or give assistance.

text: What speed limit applies in a posted school zone when children are present and the approaching limit is 50 km/h?
explanation: When children are present in such a school zone, the maximum speed is 30 km/h. The rule applies whatever the time of day.

text: In a school zone where the approaching speed limit is higher than 50 km/h, what is the maximum speed when children are present?
explanation: For school zones with an approaching limit higher than 50 km/h, the school-zone maximum is 50 km/h when children are present.

text: What should you do when weather or traffic makes the posted speed unsafe?

options.text: Drive slower than the posted limit
Use hazard lights and maintain speed

explanation: The posted limit is a maximum for ideal conditions. Slow down when weather, visibility, traffic, or road surface conditions require it.

text: What is a good minimum following distance in normal conditions?

options.text: At least two seconds behind the vehicle ahead
Exactly one car length at any speed
Close enough to read the plate

explanation: Use at least a two-second following distance in good conditions. Increase the space when conditions are poor.

text: When should you increase your following distance?

options.text: In rain, snow, darkness, heavy traffic, or when following large vehicles
Never if you have ABS brakes

explanation: Poor visibility, slippery roads, heavy traffic, and large vehicles all increase stopping needs. More space gives you time to react.

text: Where is parking normally prohibited?

options.text: In front of a fire hydrant
Beside a curb where signs allow it

explanation: Never park where you block emergency access, such as in front of a fire hydrant. Obey all posted parking controls.

text: Before opening a vehicle door on the traffic side, what should you do?

options.text: Open it quickly so others react
Only check the mirror after opening

explanation: Check mirrors and shoulder before opening a door. Opening into a cyclist or vehicle path can cause a serious collision.

text: When parking on a hill facing downhill, which way should you turn your front wheels?
explanation: Turn wheels toward the curb when parking downhill. This helps the curb stop the vehicle if it rolls.

text: When is passing another vehicle unsafe?

options.text: When you cannot see far enough ahead
On a straight road with a clear passing lane
When markings and signs allow it

explanation: Do not pass unless you have enough clear sight distance and the law allows it. If in doubt, wait.

text: What should you do before changing lanes to pass?

options.text: Signal, check mirrors, check blind spot, then move when safe
Assume other drivers will slow down

explanation: A safe lane change includes signalling, mirror checks, a shoulder check, and moving only when the space is clear.

text: What should you do if another vehicle is stopped at a crosswalk for a pedestrian?

options.text: Do not pass the stopped vehicle
Pass only if you cannot see the pedestrian

explanation: Never pass a vehicle stopped at a crosswalk for a pedestrian. A pedestrian may be hidden from your view.

text: When entering a highway from an acceleration lane, what should you do?

options.text: Match traffic speed, signal, and merge when safe
Stop at the end of the lane

explanation: Use the acceleration lane to build speed, signal, and merge into a safe gap. Avoid stopping unless traffic conditions require it.

text: What should you do if you miss your highway exit?

options.text: Make a U-turn through the median

explanation: Never back up or make sudden illegal moves on a highway. Continue to the next exit and return safely.

text: Why should you avoid driving for long periods beside another vehicle on a multi-lane road?

options.text: It can block escape space and keep you in a blind spot
It saves fuel only at low speed

explanation: Keeping space around your vehicle gives you options if something changes. Avoid lingering in another driver’s blind spot.

text: What does a solid yellow line on your side of the centre line usually mean?

options.text: The lane is for buses only

explanation: A solid yellow line on your side means passing is restricted. Wait for legal markings and clear sight distance.

text: What do white lane lines normally separate?

options.text: Traffic moving in the same direction

explanation: White lane lines separate traffic moving in the same direction. Yellow markings are used to separate opposing traffic.

text: What does a painted stop line show?

options.text: Where to stop at a sign or signal

explanation: A stop line marks where drivers should stop for a stop sign, traffic signal, or crossing control. Stop before the line.

text: What should you check before driving?

options.text: Mirrors, seat, lights, tires, and that passengers are secure

explanation: A safe pre-drive check includes visibility, controls, lights, tires, and occupant safety. Small checks prevent avoidable problems.

text: Why should all occupants wear seat belts?

options.text: They greatly reduce injury risk in a collision
They are only useful on highways
They are optional in the back seat

explanation: Seat belts help keep occupants in place and reduce injury in crashes. Airbags are designed to work with seat belts, not replace them.

text: What should you do if your brakes fail?

options.text: Pump the brake pedal, shift lower, and use the parking brake carefully
Turn off the engine immediately at highway speed

explanation: If brakes fail, try pumping the pedal, shift to a lower gear, and apply the parking brake carefully while steering to a safe place.

text: What should you do first when rain, snow, or fog reduces visibility?

options.text: Slow down and increase following distance

explanation: Poor visibility requires lower speed and more space. Use appropriate lights and avoid following too closely.

text: Why are bridges and overpasses hazardous in winter?

options.text: They can freeze before other road surfaces

explanation: Bridges and overpasses are exposed to cold air above and below, so they can become icy sooner than regular pavement.

text: What is hydroplaning?

options.text: When tires ride on water and lose contact with the road
When steering locks at low speed

explanation: Hydroplaning happens when water builds between tires and the road. Slow down in heavy rain and avoid sudden steering or braking.

text: What should you do when meeting oncoming traffic at night?

options.text: Dim high beams to avoid glare
Keep high beams on to see better

explanation: Dim your high beams for oncoming traffic so you do not blind other drivers. Look toward the right edge if glare bothers you.

text: Why should you reduce speed at night?

options.text: You can only see as far as your headlights reach
Other vehicles have no right of way

explanation: At night, visibility is limited by headlights and glare. Drive at a speed that lets you stop within the distance you can see.

text: What is the safest rule about driving after drinking or using impairing drugs?
explanation: Alcohol and drugs impair judgment, reaction time, and coordination. The safe choice is not to drive.

text: Can prescription or over-the-counter medicine affect driving?

options.text: Yes, some medicines can impair driving

explanation: Some legal medicines can cause drowsiness or slowed reactions. Read labels and ask a health professional when unsure.

text: Why is planning a sober ride before going out important?

options.text: Impaired judgment can make bad choices more likely later
It lets you ignore licence restrictions
It is only needed for long trips

explanation: Impairment makes it harder to judge risk later. Plan a sober ride, transit, taxi, or staying over before drinking or using impairing drugs.

text: What does a regulatory sign tell drivers?

options.text: A law or rule they must obey

explanation: Regulatory signs state legal requirements such as stop, yield, speed, parking, and movement controls. Drivers must obey them.

text: What do warning signs normally tell drivers?

options.text: A hazard or change in road conditions ahead

explanation: Warning signs prepare drivers for hazards such as curves, crossings, lane changes, and slippery surfaces. Slow and adjust early.

text: What do orange signs usually warn about?
explanation: Orange signs identify construction and work zones. Watch for workers, equipment, lane shifts, and temporary traffic control.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A red octagon means stop. Come to a full stop and proceed only when it is safe.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A yield sign means slow down or stop if needed and give the right of way to traffic or pedestrians.
imageAlt: Red and white triangular yield sign

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A posted speed sign shows the legal maximum speed in good conditions. Drive slower when conditions require it.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A no entry sign marks a road or lane you must not enter. Choose another legal route.

text: What does this sign mean?

options.text: One-way traffic in the arrow direction

explanation: A one-way sign shows traffic is allowed only in the direction of the arrow. Do not drive against it.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A no parking sign means you cannot park in that area. You may only stop briefly if the law and signs permit.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A no passing sign means you must not pass other vehicles in that zone. Wait until passing is legally allowed again.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: This warning sign tells drivers a traffic signal is ahead. Be prepared to stop if the light changes.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A stop-ahead sign warns that a stop sign is coming up. Begin slowing early.

text: What does this sign indicate?
explanation: School signs warn drivers to watch for children and school crossings. Slow down and be ready to stop.

text: What does this sign indicate?
explanation: A pedestrian sign warns that people may be crossing or walking nearby. Watch carefully and yield where required.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A playground sign warns that children may be near the road. Slow down and scan both sides.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A railway warning sign means train tracks are ahead. Slow down, look, listen, and be ready to stop.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A slippery-when-wet sign warns that traction may be reduced. Slow down and avoid sudden braking or steering.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A deer crossing sign warns that wildlife may enter the road. Reduce speed and scan the shoulders.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: This sign warns that a divided highway begins ahead. Keep to the correct side of the divider.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A lane-ends sign warns that your lane will end or narrow. Check mirrors and merge safely.

text: What does this sign warn you about?
explanation: A merge sign warns that traffic streams join. Adjust speed and create space.

text: What does this sign mean?
explanation: A two-way traffic sign warns that vehicles travel in both directions on the road. Stay right and watch for oncoming traffic.

text: What does this sign warn you about?

options.text: Truck entrance or trucks crossing ahead

explanation: This sign warns that trucks may be entering or crossing the road. Slow down and be ready for large vehicles.

text: What does this orange sign mean?
explanation: Orange signs identify road work and construction areas. Watch for workers, equipment, and temporary directions.

text: What does this sign identify?
explanation: A hospital sign gives service information. It helps drivers locate hospital facilities.

text: What does this sign identify?
explanation: The wheelchair symbol identifies accessible parking or facilities. Respect posted accessible-use rules.

text: What does this sign identify?
explanation: A route marker identifies a numbered highway or route. Use it for navigation and trip planning.

text: Which sign shape usually tells you to stop?
explanation: The stop sign is a red octagon. Shape recognition helps when visibility or distance makes words hard to read.

text: Which sign means you must give right of way and stop if necessary?
explanation: A yield sign requires you to give right of way. Stop if traffic or pedestrians make stopping necessary.
imageAlt: Red and white triangular yield sign

text: Which sign warns that the road surface may be dangerous after rain?
explanation: The slippery-when-wet sign warns of reduced traction. Slow down before curves and avoid sudden moves.

text: Which sign should make you watch for children near the roadway?
explanation: A playground sign warns that children may be nearby. Reduce speed and prepare to stop.

text: Which sign tells you that a traffic lane will join your lane or road?
explanation: A merge sign warns that traffic flows are joining. Adjust speed and leave space for vehicles to merge.

text: You see an orange diamond sign ahead. What should you expect?

options.text: A work zone or construction condition

explanation: Orange warning signs are used for construction or work zones. Be ready for workers, equipment, lane changes, and lower speeds.

text: What should you do when you see this railway sign?

options.text: Slow down, look and listen for trains, and be ready to stop
Stop only if the crossing has gates
Drive around lowered gates if no train is visible

explanation: Railway warning signs mean tracks are ahead. Always look and listen, and never drive around lowered gates.

text: What does a lane-ends warning require you to plan for?

options.text: A safe merge before the lane runs out
A full stop in your lane

explanation: A lane-ends sign gives advance notice to merge. Signal, check mirrors and blind spot, and move into a safe gap.

text: What does this sign tell drivers about traffic direction?

options.text: Only bicycles may use the road

explanation: Two-way traffic signs warn drivers to expect oncoming traffic. Keep right and avoid drifting over the centre line.

text: What does this sign mean for a driver approaching it?

options.text: Do not enter this road or lane

explanation: No entry means drivers must not enter that road or lane. It often protects one-way streets, ramps, or restricted areas.
